skill.name: nature-data
skill.description: Prepare Nature-style Data Availability statements, repository plans, dataset citations, and FAIR metadata checks for research manuscripts, with support for Chinese author notes.
skill.summary: Draft and audit Nature-ready data availability language, repository choices, and FAIR metadata.
skill.author: Yuan1z skill, refactored into static/dynamic layers

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported 125 issues, but review found markdown documentation, policy templates, and official source citations rather than executable behavior. Backtick, weak-crypto, reconnaissance, and entropy findings are false positives; the only retained risk factor is benign hardcoded official-policy URLs used as references.

low_findings.title: Markdown backticks misclassified as shell execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks in prose, tables, and fenced text templates as Ruby or shell execution. These locations contain documentation, file names, controlled vocabulary, or example manuscript wording, with no command runner, script, or user-input execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is markdown content and manuscript templates. No executable language runtime, shell invocation, or command API is present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak-cryptography detections are publication-data vocabulary
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged ordinary data-publication terms and YAML description text as weak cryptography. The reviewed locations discuss Data Availability, repository identifiers, source data, FAIR metadata, and manuscript policy; they do not call MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any cryptographic API.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found policy prose and metadata vocabulary only. There is no cryptographic implementation or security-sensitive hashing behavior in these files.

low_findings.title: System-reconnaissance detections are domain terms
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged words that look like system reconnaissance terms, but the reviewed lines are about corresponding authors, repository categories, and dataset identifiers. No host inspection, environment discovery, or filesystem enumeration logic exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is academic data-sharing guidance. The files contain no commands or APIs capable of collecting system information.

low_findings.title: Official source URLs are benign references
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are present, but they are citations to Springer Nature, Nature, Scientific Data, FAIR, and DataCite policy sources. The skill does not fetch these URLs or transmit data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are listed under an official sources section. No network client or automated request behavior is present in the skill files.

low_findings.title: README entropy finding is caused by multilingual documentation
low_findings.description: The high-entropy heuristic on README.md is explained by Chinese UTF-8 text and a box-drawing file tree. The file is readable documentation, not binary, packed, encrypted, or obfuscated content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual inspection shows plain markdown documentation. The file command reports UTF-8 text, and no encoded payload or executable blob was found.

content.value_statement: Research authors often need precise data availability wording before submission. This skill helps draft statements, map datasets to access routes, and check FAIR metadata.
actual_capabilities: Drafts Nature-style Data Availability statements from author notes.
Classifies datasets as public, controlled, supplementary, third-party, request-based, or not applicable.
Recommends repository and identifier strategies such as DOI, accession, Handle, or ARK.
Builds FAIR metadata and README checklists for dataset deposits.
Converts Chinese data-sharing notes into submission-ready English wording.
Flags missing access routes, licences, embargo details, and data-use conditions.
limitations: Does not verify live DOI, accession, repository, or journal policy pages.
Does not replace institutional legal, ethics, or data access review.
Does not invent repository records, licences, approvals, or identifiers.
Focuses on data availability and metadata, not full manuscript editing.

use_cases.title: Prepare a manuscript data section
use_cases.description: Draft a ready-to-paste Data Availability statement and identify missing repository details before submission.

use_cases.title: Audit repository readiness
use_cases.description: Check dataset records, README content, licences, and identifiers against FAIR metadata expectations.

use_cases.title: Convert Chinese author notes
use_cases.description: Turn Chinese descriptions of public, restricted, or request-based data into accurate English submission language.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a basic statement
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Nature-style Data Availability statement from these notes: dataset type, repository, accession or DOI, source data files, and any restrictions.

prompt_templates.title: Classify access routes
prompt_templates.prompt: Classify each dataset in my manuscript as public repository, controlled access, supplementary data, reused public data, third-party restricted, request-based, or not applicable.

prompt_templates.title: Review FAIR metadata
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this dataset deposit plan for FAIR metadata gaps. Check identifiers, README fields, file organization, licence, provenance, and citation details.

prompt_templates.title: Convert Chinese notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert my Chinese data availability notes into Nature-ready English. Preserve a short Chinese checklist of missing fields I must confirm.

output_examples.input: Author notes list RNA-seq data, figure source data, and a public reference dataset.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a Data Availability statement that separates generated data, source data files, and reused public data. It also lists repository and citation actions.

output_examples.input: A manuscript uses human participant data that cannot be openly shared.
output_examples.output: The skill drafts controlled-access wording with restriction reasons, access review route, required approvals, and public metadata actions.

output_examples.input: Chinese notes say the data can be requested from the corresponding author.
output_examples.output: The skill explains why that wording is weak, drafts a stronger English route, and provides a Chinese checklist for missing access conditions.

best_practices: List every dataset that supports main or supplementary results before drafting.
Use stable repository identifiers instead of temporary links or private folders.
State restriction reasons, access reviewers, and conditions when data cannot be public.
anti_patterns: Writing that all data are available on request without a specific restriction.
Inventing accession numbers, repository records, licences, or embargo dates.
Using one generic statement for datasets with different access routes.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces Data Availability wording, repository actions, dataset citation checks, FAIR metadata gaps, and Chinese confirmation notes when useful.

faq.question: Can it choose a repository for my dataset?
faq.answer: It can recommend repository types and identifier strategies. You must confirm the final repository requirements and create the actual record.

faq.question: Does it support restricted human data?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps describe privacy, consent, access committees, data-use agreements, and public metadata without exposing sensitive data.

faq.question: Can I use available on request wording?
faq.answer: Use it only when a real legal, ethical, commercial, or third-party restriction prevents public sharing. The statement still needs an access process.

faq.question: Does it verify identifiers online?
faq.answer: No. It can flag fields to verify, but authors must check DOI, accession, reviewer link, and journal pages themselves.

faq.question: How does the Chinese support work?
faq.answer: Users can provide Chinese notes. The skill drafts English submission text and adds concise Chinese questions for unresolved fields.
